title: The Nervous System and Mindful Returning: How Coming Back to Yourself Heals Trauma
excerpt: Discover how mindful returning to your body regulates the nervous system and supports trauma healing through body-based awareness and presence.
body:
Understanding Trauma Beyond the Mind

Trauma isn’t just a psychological event stored in your memory—it’s deeply embedded in the nervous system. Bessel van der Kolk, in his seminal work The Body Keeps the Score, emphasizes that traumatic experiences disrupt our nervous system’s ability to regulate itself, leading to persistent patterns of dysregulation. These patterns manifest as hyperarousal, dissociation, or shutdown, none of which simply dissolve through talking alone.

To truly heal, we need to return to our body—to reconnect with the sensations, rhythms, and signals our nervous system is constantly sending. This process is often referred to as mindful returning, a grounded, somatic practice that helps us come back to ourselves in the present moment.

The Nervous System’s Role in Trauma

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory provides a clear framework for understanding how the nervous system reacts to threat and trauma. According to Porges, our autonomic nervous system has three primary states: the social engagement system (ventral vagal), the fight-or-flight response (sympathetic), and the freeze or shutdown response (dorsal vagal). Trauma disrupts our ability to access the social engagement system, trapping us in states of defense.

This dysregulation explains why survivors often feel stuck in either hyperarousal or numbness. The nervous system's default to survival mode overrides the capacity to feel safe and connected. Mindful returning helps by creating safety cues that activate the ventral vagal system, inviting regulation and balance.

What Is Mindful Returning?

Mindful returning is a deliberate, body-centered practice of sensing and orienting back to the present moment. It’s not about pushing away discomfort or forcing relaxation, but rather about allowing what is felt, observing it with curiosity, and gently guiding attention back to bodily experience.

Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, describes this as “tracking sensation” — paying attention to subtle bodily cues like breath, muscle tone, and temperature. This somatic awareness is the doorway to nervous system regulation.

Key Components of Mindful Returning:

  • Embodied Awareness: Noticing physical sensations without judgment.
  • Grounding: Feeling connection to the body and environment.
  • Orientation: Using the five senses to re-establish presence.
  • Gentle Curiosity: Observing sensations with openness instead of avoidance.

How Mindful Returning Heals Trauma

The healing power of mindful returning comes from its ability to restore nervous system regulation and interrupt trauma-triggered patterns. Peter Levine, creator of Somatic Experiencing, emphasizes that trauma is stored as incomplete defensive responses that are frozen in the body. Mindful returning helps complete these responses by providing a safe container for sensations to be felt and processed.

By cultivating body awareness, the nervous system learns it can tolerate sensation without being overwhelmed, which fosters resilience and flexibility. This neurobiological shift is the foundation for trauma healing.

"Healing trauma means reconnecting with the body and its sensations in a way that cultivates safety and presence, not overwhelm." — Bessel van der Kolk

Practical Steps for Mindful Returning

Ready to start coming back to yourself? Here are accessible steps based on research and clinical practice:

  1. Pause and Breathe: When triggered, stop what you’re doing and take slow, deep breaths. Deb Dana, a leader in Polyvagal-informed therapy, recommends breathing at a pace that feels calming to your nervous system.
  2. Scan Your Body: Notice areas of tension, warmth, or numbness without trying to change them.
  3. Orient to Your Environment: Look around and name five things you see, four things you feel, three things you hear, two things you smell, and one thing you taste. This sensory engagement helps activate the ventral vagal system.
  4. Allow Sensations: Instead of pushing away discomfort, acknowledge it. Ask yourself, "What does this sensation want me to know?"
  5. Use Movement: Gentle rocking, stretching, or swaying can help discharge trapped energy and promote regulation.
  6. Practice Regularly: Incorporate these exercises daily to strengthen your nervous system’s capacity for safety and presence.

Integrating Mindful Returning with Other Healing Modalities

Mindful returning doesn’t replace therapy but complements it. Francine Shapiro’s Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR), for example, uses bilateral stimulation to access and reprocess traumatic memories while maintaining mindful awareness.

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model also benefits from somatic awareness by helping individuals notice how different internal parts feel in the body. Gabor Maté highlights that understanding the body’s signals is key to addressing addiction and emotional dysregulation.

Combining mindful returning with these approaches creates a more integrated healing process that addresses trauma on multiple levels: mind, body, and nervous system.

Why Self-Directed Healing Needs the Nervous System

Many people feel stuck after traditional talk therapy because trauma is not just a cognitive issue—it’s somatic. Dan Siegel emphasizes the importance of integration: connecting mind, body, and relationships to foster well-being.

Self-directed healing that includes mindful returning offers a way to work with your nervous system in real time, helping you break free from trauma’s hold and reclaim your capacity for safety, connection, and joy.

Final Thoughts

Healing trauma isn’t about erasing memories or forcing change; it’s about coming back to yourself—your body, your sensations, your nervous system—and cultivating safety in that presence. Mindful returning is a powerful, science-backed practice that helps you regulate your nervous system, complete frozen responses, and build resilience.

As you engage with your body with gentle curiosity and patience, you open the door to deep transformation. Your nervous system remembers how to come home.

faqs.q: Why does trauma affect the nervous system so deeply?
faqs.a: Trauma triggers survival responses in the nervous system, causing it to become dysregulated and stuck in states of fight, flight, or freeze, which can persist long after the traumatic event.

faqs.q: How is mindful returning different from mindfulness meditation?
faqs.a: Mindful returning specifically focuses on somatic awareness and orienting back to bodily sensations to regulate the nervous system, whereas mindfulness meditation often involves broader awareness of thoughts and emotions.

faqs.q: Can I practice mindful returning without a therapist?
faqs.a: Yes, mindful returning is a self-directed practice that anyone can learn, though working with a trauma-informed therapist can provide additional support and guidance.

faqs.q: How long does it take to see results from mindful returning?
faqs.a: Results vary, but with consistent practice, many people notice increased regulation and reduced trauma symptoms within weeks to months.
